Then, three years ago, he developed swelling, redness, and pain in his left ear's cartilage.
They tried antibiotics for cellulitis, but that didn't help.
Increasing the prednisone dose made the ear symptoms go away, but tapering caused them to come back.
A biopsy showed perichondrial inflammation and cartilage degeneration.
This sounds a lot like relapsing polychondritis, which affects cartilage, especially in the ears.
Looking at the photos, the left ear had edema and erythema, while the right was normal.
That's classic for chondritis.
Also, he had ocular symptoms—scleritis in the left eye with redness and pain.
Scleritis is often associated with systemic diseases like relapsing polychondritis or other rheumatic conditions.
His lab tests showed high ESR and CRP, which are signs of inflammation.
He tried various treatments: methotrexate, tocilizumab, infliximab, rituximab, and cyclophosphamide.
These are all typically used for autoimmune or inflammatory conditions.
The fact that his symptoms kept coming back when they tried to taper steroids suggests a chronic underlying issue.
The pleural effusion is interesting.
He had it drained, and the fluid analysis didn't show infection or cancer.
It was attributed to ongoing inflammation.
Relapsing polychondritis can cause pleural effusions because of the systemic inflammation affecting the lungs and pleura.
He also had some issues with blood clots in his legs, which they thought were due to the underlying inflammation.
That makes sense because some autoimmune diseases can increase clotting risks.
His other symptoms, like the back pain and imaging showing a larger pleural effusion, tie back to the ongoing inflammatory process.
The fact that his ESR and CRP are still elevated despite various treatments points towards a chronic, systemic inflammatory condition.
Putting it all together: he has a history of relapsing symptoms, ear chondritis, scleritis, and pleural effusions, all of which are classic for relapsing polychondritis.
The treatments he's had, like steroids and biologics, are typical for managing such conditions.
His labs support chronic inflammation, and the lack of positive ANCA or CCP antibodies makes me think it's not something like GPA or RA, which can have similar presentations but different serologies.
So, the most likely diagnosis is relapsing polychondritis.
